verdict_reason: Removing 2.12 from the chapter and giving a writer 2.11 + 2.13 + chapter thesis, the writer would predict that the 12th-position verse opens the formal sāṅkhya statement of eternality — they could plausibly write a generic 'requirements outlive teams' analog. However, the specific signature of this verse's analog — the three-times structure (past 'predates founders' / present 'today's owners' / future 'will not pack it away'), the explicit non-commitment to whether the substrate is one or many ('whatever durable demand'), the custodianship-under-team-turnover frame as a defensibility criterion — depends on Sanskrit specifics (the past-present-future plural enumeration of 'I, you, these kings, all of us') and on the source-pack's careful preservation of the Advaita/Vishishtadvaita non-commitment. A chapter-only writer would write a flatter 'long-term thinking' verse; the verse-grounded writer goes further. PASS.
specific_signal_contributed: the three-times structure of eternality (past, present, future) as the discipline of recognition
explicit non-commitment to ontology of the substrate (one durable need, or many — both readings preserved)
custodianship reframe (the team is custodian, not owner) as the architectural-decision predicate
